cards.back: Vomiting blood; if exposed to gastric acid it may look like coffee grounds.
Difficulty swallowing.
Painful swallowing.
Excessive flatus from swallowed air.
Associated with hepatitis, biliary cirrhosis, or gallstones.
Loss of appetite.
Dark, tarry stool from upper GI bleeding.
Bright red blood, often from rectum, anus, or sigmoid.
Fatty diarrheal stool.
May indicate impaired biliary production or flow.
Midline and usually inverted.
Redness, swelling, discoloration, or lesions are abnormal.
Abdominal movement should be symmetric with no masses.
Marked distention or severe concavity is abnormal.
Sedentary lifestyle increases constipation risk; walking 30 minutes daily helps.
Balanced high-fiber diet and about 8 to 10 glasses of water per day.
Routine screening is recommended around age 50, earlier if high risk.
Common interval is every 10 years for average-risk adults.
Promote beneficial microorganisms in the GI tract.
Peristalsis is stimulated after eating.
